title: Mathematics + Financial Literacy: Class 9 — Karnataka State Board
subject: Mathematics

questions.text: A Bengaluru startup employee earns ₹6,00,000 per annum. The income tax slabs are: 0% up to ₹2,50,000; 5% from ₹2,50,001 to ₹5,00,000; 20% from ₹5,00,001 to ₹10,00,000. What is their annual income tax before any deductions?
questions.explanation: 0% on first ₹2,50,000 = ₹0. 5% on next ₹2,50,000 (₹2,50,001 to ₹5,00,000) = ₹12,500. 20% on next ₹1,00,000 (₹5,00,001 to ₹6,00,000) = ₹20,000. Total tax = ₹0 + ₹12,500 + ₹20,000 = ₹32,500. (Note: exact 2024-25 regime differs slightly). This slab system uses linear equations and percentage across intervals — your Class 9 algebra and percentage chapters.
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.text: A plot of land in Kolar shows a triangular shape with base 80 m and height 60 m. The government charges stamp duty of 5% on the land value. If land is priced at ₹2,000 per square metre, what is the total cost including stamp duty?
questions.explanation: Area of triangle = ½ × base × height = ½ × 80 × 60 = 2,400 m². Land value = 2,400 × ₹2,000 = ₹48,00,000. Stamp duty = 5% of ₹48,00,000 = ₹2,40,000. Total = ₹50,40,000. Real estate in Karnataka uses exactly this — triangular/irregular plots need area formulae (your Chapter: Areas of Plane Figures) to determine stamp duty, which is a major government revenue source.
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.text: A Mysuru shopkeeper's monthly sales data (in ₹ thousands) over 6 months is: 45, 52, 38, 60, 55, 48. The shopkeeper claims sales are 'on average' above ₹50,000. Is this claim correct, and which measure of central tendency should be used for financial reporting?

options.text: Incorrect — mean is ₹49,667 (below ₹50,000); mean is the appropriate measure for financial reporting
Correct — median is ₹51,500 (above ₹50,000); median should be used
Incorrect — range shows high variability so no average is reliable

questions.explanation: Mean = (45+52+38+60+55+48)/6 = 298/6 = 49.67 thousand = ₹49,667. The claim is slightly incorrect. For financial reporting (GST returns, bank loan applications), mean (arithmetic average) is standard. Note: Median = average of 3rd and 4th values when sorted (38, 45, 48, 52, 55, 60) = (48+52)/2 = 50,000 — the shopkeeper could legitimately use median! This connects your Statistics chapter to business reporting.
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.text: Karnataka's Namma Metro (Phase 2) is funded partly by bonds offering 7.5% compound interest per annum. If you invest ₹10,000 in these bonds, how much do you receive after 3 years?
questions.explanation: A = P(1 + r/100)ⁿ = 10000 × (1.075)³ = 10000 × 1.2423 = ₹12,423 (approx). Simple interest would give only ₹12,250. The extra ₹173 is 'interest on interest' — the power of compounding. Namma Metro bonds, Fixed Deposits, and government savings schemes all use compound interest. Your Chapter on Compound Interest shows why starting to invest early is powerful.
questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.text: A Class 9 student in Shivamogga wants to save ₹50,000 for a laptop in 2 years. A bank offers 6% compound interest per annum. How much should she deposit now (present value calculation)?
questions.explanation: PV = FV / (1+r/100)ⁿ = 50,000 / (1.06)² = 50,000 / 1.1236 ≈ ₹44,499 ≈ ₹44,500. This 'present value' concept is used by every bank, insurance company, and financial planner in India. If she deposits ₹44,500 today at 6% CI, it grows to ₹50,000 in 2 years. Your Compound Interest chapter is the foundation of all financial planning!
questions.subject: Mathematics
